edges.description: FAH block causes upstream toxic tyrosine-pathway metabolites to accumulate.
Defective tyrosine catabolism contributes to elevated tyrosine, particularly during HPD inhibition with nitisinone.
intermediate_mechanisms: Impaired terminal tyrosine catabolism and nitisinone treatment both increase tyrosine-pathway substrate burden.

edges.description: FAH deficiency directly increases the toxic substrate fumarylacetoacetate.
Toxic tyrosine intermediates produce the pathognomonic metabolite succinylacetone.
intermediate_mechanisms: Accumulated fumarylacetoacetate and maleylacetoacetate generate succinylacetone through alternative routes.

edges.description: FAA reactivity drives mitochondrial injury and caspase-mediated hepatocyte apoptosis.
Succinylacetone accumulation perturbs ALAD-linked heme biosynthesis and contributes to porphyria-like crises.
Tyrosine-pathway metabolite toxicity causes renal tubular injury and Fanconi-like tubulopathy.
intermediate_mechanisms: Toxic tyrosine-pathway metabolites damage renal tissue as well as liver.

edges.description: Toxic metabolite effects on the nervous system and long-term treatment factors contribute to neurocognitive risk.
Residual FAH deficiency leaves chronic pro-oncogenic hepatic stress despite upstream metabolic blockade by NTBC.
intermediate_mechanisms: Residual FAH deficiency leaves hepatic stress and regenerative programs despite upstream HPD blockade.
edges.target: Persistent hepatocarcinogenic programs under NTBC therapy

edges.description: Caspase-mediated hepatocyte death causes acute hepatic failure in severe or untreated HT-1.
Acute hepatic failure impairs coagulation factor synthesis.
intermediate_mechanisms: Hepatocyte apoptosis causes hepatic synthetic failure.

edges.description: Hepatic injury and regenerative stress can present with enlarged liver.
Toxic injury to proximal tubule epithelium produces Fanconi-like renal tubular dysfunction.
Renal Fanconi syndrome causes phosphate wasting and secondary rickets.
intermediate_mechanisms: Proximal tubular phosphate wasting causes hypophosphatemic rickets.

edges.description: Chronic hepatic injury in untreated HT-1 produces fibrosis.
Chronic hepatic injury and fibrosis can progress to cirrhosis.
Chronic liver injury and regeneration increase HCC risk.
intermediate_mechanisms: Chronic hepatocyte injury and regeneration create pro-oncogenic stress.

edges.description: Chronic liver disease and metabolic instability impair growth.
Advanced hepatic disease can cause peripheral edema or ascites.
intermediate_mechanisms: Liver synthetic dysfunction and portal-hypertensive physiology can cause fluid retention.

edges.description: ALAD inhibition increases ALA accumulation.
Porphyria-like crises in HT-1 can include peripheral neuropathy.
intermediate_mechanisms: ALA accumulation produces porphyria-like neurovisceral crises.

edges.description: Porphyria-like neurologic crises in HT-1 can include abdominal pain.
intermediate_mechanisms: ALA accumulation produces porphyria-like neurovisceral crises.

edges.description: Porphyria-like neurologic crises in HT-1 can include altered mental status.
intermediate_mechanisms: ALA accumulation produces porphyria-like neurovisceral crises.

edges.description: Severe neurologic crises in HT-1 can include respiratory failure.
intermediate_mechanisms: ALA accumulation produces porphyria-like neurovisceral crises.

edges.description: Residual hepatic disease programs under NTBC support sustained HCC risk.
intermediate_mechanisms: Persistent liver-disease and HCC-associated transcriptional programs remain under NTBC.
edges.source: Persistent hepatocarcinogenic programs under NTBC therapy

edges.description: Persistent HCC risk motivates AFP surveillance in HT-1.
intermediate_mechanisms: AFP is used as a marker of hepatic disease activity and HCC surveillance.
edges.source: Persistent hepatocarcinogenic programs under NTBC therapy

edges.description: AFP elevation is tracked as a marker of disease activity and malignant transformation risk.
intermediate_mechanisms: Hepatic injury and HCC risk drive AFP monitoring.
edges.source: Persistent hepatocarcinogenic programs under NTBC therapy

edges.description: Elevated succinylacetone reports the toxic tyrosine-intermediate branch and is the pathognomonic HT-1 screening marker.
Succinylacetone presence reports the ALAD-inhibition branch that drives ALA accumulation and porphyria-like crises.
Tyrosine is a less specific screening signal for HT-1 than succinylacetone, so its interpretation is threshold- and assay-dependent.
Rising, slowly decreasing, or non-normalizing AFP tracks residual HCC-risk biology during NTBC-treated HT-1.
edges.source: Persistent hepatocarcinogenic programs under NTBC therapy

edges.description: AFP is monitored because a rise or persistently abnormal decline pattern predicts liver cancer in HT-1.
Urinary ALA accumulation reports succinylacetone-mediated ALAD inhibition in the heme-biosynthesis branch.
FAA accumulation reports the proximal toxic-metabolite block downstream of FAH deficiency.
FAA is the chemical substrate whose accumulation predicts the mitochondrial apoptosis branch in HT-1 models.

nodes.description: Porphyria-like neurologic crises in HT-1 can include abdominal pain, linking succinylacetone-mediated heme-biosynthesis inhibition to an acute neurovisceral manifestation.

evidence.reference_title: Tyrosinemia Type I.
evidence.snippet: neurologic crises lasting one to seven days that can include change in mental status, abdominal pain, peripheral neuropathy, and/or respiratory failure

nodes.description: Acute hepatic decompensation is a hallmark of untreated or severe HT-1, driven by FAA-mediated oxidative damage and caspase-dependent apoptosis of hepatocytes. In untreated acute-form HT-1, liver failure typically presents in the first months of life.

evidence.reference_title: Diagnosis and treatment of tyrosinemia type I: a US and Canadian consensus group review and recommendations.
evidence.snippet: Tyrosinemia type I (hepatorenal tyrosinemia, HT-1) is an autosomal recessive condition resulting in hepatic failure with comorbidities involving the renal and neurologic systems

evidence.reference_title: Decoding hepatorenal tyrosinemia type 1: Unraveling the impact of early detection, NTBC, and the role of liver transplantation.
evidence.snippet: Patients failing medical treatment (eg, due to non-adherence), and who develop acute liver failure (ALF), have HCC or evidence of histologically proven dysplastic liver nodule(s)

evidence.reference_title: Tyrosinemia type I treated by NTBC: how does AFP predict liver cancer?
evidence.snippet: Apart from a rise of AFP, a slow AFP decrease, and never normalizing levels of AFP are important predictors of liver cancer development in further life.

readouts.interpretation: Rising, slowly decreasing, or non-normalizing AFP tracks residual HCC-risk biology during NTBC-treated HT-1.
readouts.target: Persistent hepatocarcinogenic programs under NTBC therapy

readouts.interpretation: AFP is monitored because a rise or persistently abnormal decline pattern predicts liver cancer in HT-1.

nodes.description: Untreated or late-treated HT-1 produces chronic hepatic injury with regeneration, fibrosis, cirrhosis, and malignant transformation risk. Continuous NTBC reduces acute toxicity but does not restore FAH activity, so residual liver-disease and HCC-associated programs can persist.

evidence.reference_title: A False-Negative Newborn Screen for Tyrosinemia Type 1-Need for Re-Evaluation of Newborn Screening with Succinylacetone.
evidence.snippet: Undiagnosed and untreated tyrosinemia type 1 (TT1) individuals carry a significant risk for developing liver fibrosis, cirrhosis and hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC).

evidence.reference_title: Hereditary Tyrosinemia Type 1 Mice under Continuous Nitisinone Treatment Display Remnants of an Uncorrected Liver Disease Phenotype.
evidence.snippet: The differentially expressed genes were enriched in toxicological gene classes related to liver disease, liver damage, liver regeneration and liver cancer, in particular HCC.

nodes.description: Hepatic synthetic dysfunction leads to coagulopathy, which is one of the earliest signs of liver failure in acute-form HT-1.

evidence.reference_title: Diagnosis and treatment of tyrosinemia type I: a US and Canadian consensus group review and recommendations.
evidence.snippet: Tyrosinemia type I (hepatorenal tyrosinemia, HT-1) is an autosomal recessive condition resulting in hepatic failure

evidence.reference_title: Hereditary tyrosinemia and the heme biosynthetic pathway. Profound inhibition of delta-aminolevulinic acid dehydratase activity by succinylacetone.
evidence.snippet: patients with this hereditary disease excrete excessive amounts of delta-aminolevulinic acid (ALA) in urine

readouts.interpretation: Urinary ALA accumulation reports succinylacetone-mediated ALAD inhibition in the heme-biosynthesis branch.

nodes.description: Low-tyrosine, low-phenylalanine diet is essential in combination with NTBC therapy, since NTBC causes upstream accumulation of tyrosine. Specialized medical formulas provide essential amino acids while restricting tyrosine and phenylalanine intake.

evidence.reference_title: Diagnosis and treatment of tyrosinemia type I: a US and Canadian consensus group review and recommendations.
evidence.snippet: There was strong consensus in favor of NBS for HT-1, using blood succinylacetone as a marker, followed by diagnostic confirmation and early treatment with NTBC and diet.

evidence.reference_title: Decoding hepatorenal tyrosinemia type 1: Unraveling the impact of early detection, NTBC, and the role of liver transplantation.
evidence.snippet: NTBC combined with dietary therapy, if initiated early, can provide liver transplant (LT) free survival and reduce the risk of hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC).

nodes.description: Peripheral edema and ascites may occur due to hepatic synthetic failure with hypoalbuminemia.

evidence.reference_title: Hereditary Tyrosinemia Type-1 With Late Presentation: A Case Report.
evidence.snippet: We present a case of HT-1 in a three-year-old female child characterized by abdominal distension, facial edema, lower limb edema, and an enlarged liver with parenchymal disease.

nodes.description: Alpha-fetoprotein (AFP) is markedly elevated in HT-1 and serves as both a diagnostic and monitoring biomarker. AFP should decrease after NTBC initiation; rising or persistently elevated AFP raises suspicion for hepatocellular carcinoma or treatment non-adherence.

evidence.reference_title: Tyrosinemia type I treated by NTBC: how does AFP predict liver cancer?
evidence.snippet: A rise of alpha-fetoprotein (AFP) is an indicator of liver cancer.

nodes.description: Neurologic crises in HT-1 can include change in mental status, captured here as encephalopathy in the porphyria-like crisis branch.

evidence.reference_title: Tyrosinemia Type I.
evidence.snippet: neurologic crises lasting one to seven days that can include change in mental status, abdominal pain, peripheral neuropathy, and/or respiratory failure

nodes.description: Fumarylacetoacetate directly triggers mitochondrial outer membrane permeabilization with release of cytochrome c, activating the caspase cascade and inducing hepatocyte apoptosis. Caspase inhibitors were shown to prevent liver failure in FAH-deficient mouse models, confirming the causal role of the intrinsic apoptotic pathway.

evidence.reference_title: Hepatocyte injury in tyrosinemia type 1 is induced by fumarylacetoacetate and is inhibited by caspase inhibitors.
evidence.snippet: Cytochrome c was released from mitochondria prior to liver failure in the Fah-/- Hpd-/- double-mutant mice after the administration of HGA. In a cell-free system, the addition of fumarylacetoacetate induced the release of cytochrome c from the mitochondria.

evidence.reference_title: Hepatocyte injury in tyrosinemia type 1 is induced by fumarylacetoacetate and is inhibited by caspase inhibitors.
evidence.snippet: We also found that caspase inhibitors were highly effective in preventing the liver failure induced by HGA in the double-mutant mice.

evidence.reference_title: Decoding hepatorenal tyrosinemia type 1: Unraveling the impact of early detection, NTBC, and the role of liver transplantation.
evidence.snippet: Hepatorenal tyrosinemia type 1 (HT-1) is a rare autosomal recessive disease that results from a deficiency of fumaryl acetoacetate hydrolase (FAH), a critical enzyme in the catabolic pathway for tyrosine.

evidence.reference_title: FAH / tyrosinemia type I (Definitive)
evidence.snippet: FAH | HGNC:3579 | tyrosinemia type I | MONDO:0010161 | AR | Definitive

nodes.description: Over 100 pathogenic variants in FAH have been reported worldwide. The IVS12+5G>A splice site mutation is the most common variant in the French-Canadian population of Quebec. Various missense, nonsense, splice-site, and frameshift mutations have been identified across diverse ethnic groups.
Poor growth and failure to thrive occur in untreated or poorly controlled HT-1 due to chronic liver disease, renal dysfunction, and metabolic instability.

evidence.reference_title: Tyrosinemia Type I.
evidence.snippet: liver dysfunction and renal tubular dysfunction associated with growth failure and rickets.

evidence.reference_title: Hepatocyte injury in tyrosinemia type 1 is induced by fumarylacetoacetate and is inhibited by caspase inhibitors.
evidence.snippet: the addition of fumarylacetoacetate induced the release of cytochrome c from the mitochondria.

readouts.interpretation: FAA accumulation reports the proximal toxic-metabolite block downstream of FAH deficiency.
FAA is the chemical substrate whose accumulation predicts the mitochondrial apoptosis branch in HT-1 models.

nodes.description: Loss of FAH activity blocks the terminal enzymatic step of tyrosine catabolism.

evidence.reference_title: Decoding hepatorenal tyrosinemia type 1: Unraveling the impact of early detection, NTBC, and the role of liver transplantation.
evidence.snippet: Hepatorenal tyrosinemia type 1 (HT-1) is a rare autosomal recessive disease that results from a deficiency of fumaryl acetoacetate hydrolase (FAH), a critical enzyme in the catabolic pathway for tyrosine.

nodes.description: Cirrhosis develops progressively in untreated HT-1 as a consequence of chronic hepatocyte injury and regeneration. A case of false-negative NBS presented at age 9 with HCC in a cirrhotic liver.

evidence.reference_title: A False-Negative Newborn Screen for Tyrosinemia Type 1-Need for Re-Evaluation of Newborn Screening with Succinylacetone.
evidence.snippet: A nine-year-old boy presented with HCC in a cirrhotic liver.

evidence.reference_title: Hereditary Tyrosinemia Type-1 With Late Presentation: A Case Report.
evidence.snippet: A liver biopsy confirmed mixed nodular cirrhosis, and a subsequent whole exome sequencing revealed autosomal recessive inheritance of tyrosinemia type 1.

nodes.description: Progressive liver fibrosis and cirrhosis develop in untreated or late-treated HT-1. Even false-negative NBS cases have presented with cirrhosis years later.

evidence.reference_title: A False-Negative Newborn Screen for Tyrosinemia Type 1-Need for Re-Evaluation of Newborn Screening with Succinylacetone.
evidence.snippet: Undiagnosed and untreated tyrosinemia type 1 (TT1) individuals carry a significant risk for developing liver fibrosis, cirrhosis and hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC).

nodes.description: HCC is the most feared long-term complication of HT-1. Risk persists even under NTBC therapy, necessitating lifelong surveillance with alpha-fetoprotein monitoring and liver imaging.

evidence.reference_title: Decoding hepatorenal tyrosinemia type 1: Unraveling the impact of early detection, NTBC, and the role of liver transplantation.
evidence.snippet: NTBC combined with dietary therapy, if initiated early, can provide liver transplant (LT) free survival and reduce the risk of hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC).

nodes.description: Liver enlargement is common in HT-1 due to hepatocellular injury, regeneration nodules, and progressive liver disease.

evidence.reference_title: Hereditary Tyrosinemia Type-1 With Late Presentation: A Case Report.
evidence.snippet: We present a case of HT-1 in a three-year-old female child characterized by abdominal distension, facial edema, lower limb edema, and an enlarged liver with parenchymal disease.

nodes.description: Liver transplantation is curative for the hepatic manifestations of HT-1 but does not correct renal FAH deficiency, and residual SA production by the kidneys may persist. Indications include acute liver failure, HCC or dysplastic nodules, or failure of medical management.

evidence.reference_title: Decoding hepatorenal tyrosinemia type 1: Unraveling the impact of early detection, NTBC, and the role of liver transplantation.
evidence.snippet: Patients failing medical treatment (eg, due to non-adherence), and who develop acute liver failure (ALF), have HCC or evidence of histologically proven dysplastic liver nodule(s), or experience poor quality of life secondary to severe dietary restrictions are currently indicated for LT.

evidence.reference_title: Hereditary Tyrosinemia Type-1 With Late Presentation: A Case Report.
evidence.snippet: The patient is currently undergoing treatment with capsule nitisinone 5 mg, which inhibits the second step of tyrosine degradation to prevent tyrosinemia, along with a restricted protein diet, while awaiting liver transplantation.

nodes.description: Long-term neurocognitive assessment is recommended for HT-1 patients, as they are at risk for neurocognitive impairment even under NTBC therapy. Early intervention and educational support may improve outcomes.

evidence.reference_title: Decoding hepatorenal tyrosinemia type 1: Unraveling the impact of early detection, NTBC, and the role of liver transplantation.
evidence.snippet: They are also at risk of long-term neurocognitive impairment, which highlights the need for neurocognitive assessment and therapy.

nodes.description: Long-term neurocognitive impairment is a recognized risk in HT-1 patients, even those treated early with NTBC. Neurocognitive assessment and therapy are recommended in clinical follow-up.

evidence.reference_title: Decoding hepatorenal tyrosinemia type 1: Unraveling the impact of early detection, NTBC, and the role of liver transplantation.
evidence.snippet: They are also at risk of long-term neurocognitive impairment, which highlights the need for neurocognitive assessment and therapy.

nodes.description: NBS for HT-1 using dried blood spot succinylacetone measurement is recommended, as it is superior to tyrosine-based screening. Early identification enables pre-symptomatic NTBC initiation for optimal long-term outcomes.

evidence.reference_title: Diagnosis and treatment of tyrosinemia type I: a US and Canadian consensus group review and recommendations.
evidence.snippet: Newborn screening (NBS) utilizing blood succinylacetone as the NBS marker is superior to observing tyrosine levels as a way of identifying neonates with HT-1.

evidence.reference_title: Evaluation of Neonatal Screening Programs for Tyrosinemia Type 1 Worldwide.
evidence.snippet: Overall positive predictive values were 27.3% for SUAC, 1.2% for Tyr solely, and 90.1% for Tyr + SUAC.

evidence.reference_title: A False-Negative Newborn Screen for Tyrosinemia Type 1-Need for Re-Evaluation of Newborn Screening with Succinylacetone.
evidence.snippet: While SA was long considered to be elevated in every TT1 patient, here we present a recent false-negative SA TT1 screen.

nodes.description: Nitisinone (2-[2-nitro-4-trifluoromethylbenzoyl]-1,3-cyclohexanedione) is a potent inhibitor of 4-hydroxyphenylpyruvate dioxygenase (HPD), the second enzyme in tyrosine degradation. By blocking HPD, NTBC prevents formation of the toxic downstream metabolites FAA, MAA, and SA. Early initiation of NTBC, ideally before symptom onset via NBS identification, provides greater than 90% survival and dramatically reduces the need for liver transplantation.

evidence.reference_title: Decoding hepatorenal tyrosinemia type 1: Unraveling the impact of early detection, NTBC, and the role of liver transplantation.
evidence.snippet: has significantly improved the management of HT-1, particularly when initiated before the onset of symptoms.

evidence.reference_title: Hereditary Tyrosinemia Type 1 Mice under Continuous Nitisinone Treatment Display Remnants of an Uncorrected Liver Disease Phenotype.
evidence.snippet: Nitisinone (NTBC), a potent inhibitor of the 4-hydroxyphenylpyruvate dioxygenase (HPD) enzyme, rescues HT1 patients from severe illness and death. However, despite its demonstrated benefits, HT1 patients under continuous NTBC therapy are at risk to develop HCC

nodes.description: Porphyria-like neurovisceral crises with peripheral neuropathy result from succinylacetone-mediated inhibition of ALAD and accumulation of delta-aminolevulinic acid. Episodes mimic acute intermittent porphyria.

evidence.reference_title: Tyrosinemia Type I.
evidence.snippet: neurologic crises lasting one to seven days that can include change in mental status, abdominal pain, peripheral neuropathy, and/or respiratory failure

evidence.reference_title: Diagnosis and treatment of tyrosinemia type I: a US and Canadian consensus group review and recommendations.
evidence.snippet: Tyrosinemia type I (hepatorenal tyrosinemia, HT-1) is an autosomal recessive condition resulting in hepatic failure with comorbidities involving the renal and neurologic systems

nodes.description: Although nitisinone prevents acute toxicity by blocking upstream toxic metabolite formation, it does not restore FAH activity. Transcriptomic analysis in FAH-deficient mice under continuous NTBC revealed persistent enrichment of genes related to liver disease, liver regeneration, and hepatocellular carcinoma. This supports the ongoing HCC risk even in treated patients.
nodes.id: Persistent hepatocarcinogenic programs under NTBC therapy

evidence.reference_title: Hereditary Tyrosinemia Type 1 Mice under Continuous Nitisinone Treatment Display Remnants of an Uncorrected Liver Disease Phenotype.
evidence.snippet: NTBC does not restore the enzymatic defects inflicted by the disease nor does it cure HT1.

nodes.description: Accumulated tyrosine-pathway metabolites injure kidney tissue, especially the proximal tubule, causing a Fanconi-like renal phenotype with phosphate wasting. This renal tubular mechanism explains renal tubular dysfunction and secondary hypophosphatemic rickets in HT-1.

evidence.reference_title: Decoding hepatorenal tyrosinemia type 1: Unraveling the impact of early detection, NTBC, and the role of liver transplantation.
evidence.snippet: This leads to the accumulation of toxic metabolites such as fumaryl and maleylacetoacetate, which can damage the liver, kidneys, and nervous system.

nodes.description: Proximal renal tubular dysfunction with a Fanconi-like syndrome is a core manifestation, resulting from toxic metabolite damage to renal tubular epithelial cells. May manifest as aminoaciduria, phosphaturia, glycosuria, and renal tubular acidosis.

evidence.reference_title: Decoding hepatorenal tyrosinemia type 1: Unraveling the impact of early detection, NTBC, and the role of liver transplantation.
evidence.snippet: This leads to the accumulation of toxic metabolites such as fumaryl and maleylacetoacetate, which can damage the liver, kidneys, and nervous system.

nodes.description: Severe neurologic crises in HT-1 can include respiratory failure, consistent with the acute porphyria-like neurovisceral branch.

evidence.snippet: neurologic crises lasting one to seven days that can include change in mental status, abdominal pain, peripheral neuropathy, and/or respiratory failure

nodes.description: Hypophosphatemic rickets develops secondary to renal tubular phosphate wasting in the context of Fanconi-like tubulopathy.

evidence.reference_title: Tyrosinemia Type I.
evidence.snippet: liver dysfunction and renal tubular dysfunction associated with growth failure and rickets.

evidence.reference_title: A False-Negative Newborn Screen for Tyrosinemia Type 1-Need for Re-Evaluation of Newborn Screening with Succinylacetone.
evidence.snippet: Elevated succinylacetone (SA) is pathognomonic for TT1 and therefore often used as marker for TT1 newborn screening (NBS).

evidence.reference_title: Evaluation of Neonatal Screening Programs for Tyrosinemia Type 1 Worldwide.
evidence.snippet: Most used biomarkers were DBS SUAC (78.9%), DBS Tyrosine (Tyr; 5.3%), or DBS Tyr with second tier SUAC (15.8%). The pooled median cut-off for SUAC was 1.50 µmol/L (range 0.3-7.0 µmol/L).

readouts.interpretation: Elevated succinylacetone reports the toxic tyrosine-intermediate branch and is the pathognomonic HT-1 screening marker.
Succinylacetone presence reports the ALAD-inhibition branch that drives ALA accumulation and porphyria-like crises.

nodes.description: Succinylacetone (SA) is a competitive inhibitor of delta-aminolevulinic acid dehydratase (ALAD), blocking the heme biosynthetic pathway and causing accumulation of delta-aminolevulinic acid (ALA). This produces porphyria-like neurovisceral crises with neuropathy, similar to acute intermittent porphyria or lead poisoning.

evidence.reference_title: Hereditary tyrosinemia and the heme biosynthetic pathway. Profound inhibition of delta-aminolevulinic acid dehydratase activity by succinylacetone.
evidence.snippet: Our data indicate that succinylacetone is an extremely potent competitive inhibitor of ALA dehydratase in human as well as in animal tissues.

evidence.reference_title: Hereditary tyrosinemia and the heme biosynthetic pathway. Profound inhibition of delta-aminolevulinic acid dehydratase activity by succinylacetone.
evidence.snippet: patients with this hereditary disease excrete excessive amounts of delta-aminolevulinic acid (ALA) in urine and that certain patients have an accompanying clinical syndrome resembling that of acute intermittent porphyria (AIP).

evidence.reference_title: Hereditary tyrosinemia and the heme biosynthetic pathway. Profound inhibition of delta-aminolevulinic acid dehydratase activity by succinylacetone.
evidence.snippet: In cultured hepatocytes, succinylacetone also inhibited ALA dehydratase activity, decreased the cellular content of heme and cytochrome P-450

nodes.description: Acute and chronic supportive management includes correction of coagulopathy, electrolyte management for renal tubular dysfunction, vitamin D and phosphate supplementation for rickets, and monitoring of liver and renal function.

evidence.reference_title: Decoding hepatorenal tyrosinemia type 1: Unraveling the impact of early detection, NTBC, and the role of liver transplantation.
evidence.snippet: Children with HT-1 require frequent monitoring of liver and renal function to assess disease progression and treatment compliance.

nodes.description: FAH deficiency causes accumulation of fumarylacetoacetate (FAA) and maleylacetoacetate (MAA), with secondary formation of succinylacetone (SA). These toxic tyrosine-pathway metabolites damage the liver, kidneys, and nervous system, and FAA directly triggers mitochondrial cytochrome c release in hepatocytes.

evidence.reference_title: Decoding hepatorenal tyrosinemia type 1: Unraveling the impact of early detection, NTBC, and the role of liver transplantation.
evidence.snippet: This leads to the accumulation of toxic metabolites such as fumaryl and maleylacetoacetate, which can damage the liver, kidneys, and nervous system.

evidence.reference_title: Diagnosis and treatment of tyrosinemia type I: a US and Canadian consensus group review and recommendations.
evidence.snippet: Newborn screening (NBS) utilizing blood succinylacetone as the NBS marker is superior to observing tyrosine levels as a way of identifying neonates with HT-1.

readouts.interpretation: Tyrosine is a less specific screening signal for HT-1 than succinylacetone, so its interpretation is threshold- and assay-dependent.
